skill.name: writing-types
skill.description: Guides creation of modular TypeScript types and interfaces for the Next.js app's `srs/types` directory.
skill.summary: Create small, modular TypeScript type and interface files for a Next.js project.
skill.author: Emz1998

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged backtick usage and a weak cryptography pattern in `SKILL.md`, but review found only Markdown prose around the `srs/types` path. No shell execution, cryptographic code, data exfiltration, prompt injection, or other semantic threat was found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks in Prose
low_findings.description: The external command detections on lines 3 and 8 are false positives. The backticks format the `srs/types` directory path in Markdown text and do not execute shell commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed file is Markdown instructions only. There is no Ruby code, script block, command invocation, or user-controlled shell execution path.

low_findings.title: False Positive: No Weak Cryptography Present
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography detection on line 3 is a false positive. The line contains a natural-language description with a directory path, not a cryptographic algorithm or implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: No cryptographic API, hash function, cipher, or encoded payload appears in the reviewed content. The flagged line is descriptive Markdown frontmatter.

content.value_statement: Type files can become large and inconsistent in a growing Next.js app. This skill helps create modular TypeScript types and interfaces with small, focused files.
actual_capabilities: Guides creation of TypeScript types and interfaces for a Next.js app.
Encourages modular organization across multiple files.
Applies a maximum length rule of 50 lines per generated file.
Separates types and interfaces when the project structure supports it.
Supports consistent type placement under the specified `srs/types` directory.
limitations: Does not include validation scripts, generators, or automated refactoring tools.
Does not define naming conventions beyond modular file organization.
Does not verify whether the target directory exists in the user's project.
Does not cover runtime schemas, API clients, or database models.

use_cases.title: Create Feature Types
use_cases.description: Define focused TypeScript types for a new Next.js feature without creating oversized files.

use_cases.title: Organize Shared Interfaces
use_cases.description: Split common interfaces into small files so teams can find and update them quickly.

use_cases.title: Prepare Typed UI Work
use_cases.description: Draft clean data shapes before implementing pages, components, or hooks.

prompt_templates.title: Create Basic Types
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a small feature model.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the writing-types skill to define TypeScript types for a Next.js notes feature. Keep each file under 50 lines.

prompt_templates.title: Split Interfaces by Domain
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the writing-types skill to organize interfaces for users, notes, and tags in the `srs/types` directory. Separate files where useful.

prompt_templates.title: Review Type Modularity
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the writing-types skill to review these existing TypeScript types and suggest a modular file split under 50 lines per file.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Typed Feature Contracts
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the writing-types skill to design type contracts for a Next.js feature with list, detail, form, and API response data shapes.

output_examples.input: Create types for a notes dashboard with note cards, filters, and sort options.
output: A short plan that separates dashboard, note, filter, and sort type files.
Type and interface names that map to the feature concepts.
A reminder to keep each file below the 50-line limit.

output_examples.input: Review a large shared types file for a Next.js app.
output: A proposed split into smaller domain files.
Specific guidance on which interfaces should move together.
A concise migration order for updating imports.

best_practices: Group related types by feature or domain before creating files.
Keep each type file focused enough to stay under 50 lines.
Use clear names that match the related Next.js feature or component.
anti_patterns: Do not place unrelated interfaces in one shared catch-all file.
Do not exceed the 50-line file limit when a clean split is possible.
Do not create type files without checking how the app imports shared types.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps write modular TypeScript types and interfaces for a Next.js app.

faq.question: Does it generate runtime validation schemas?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on TypeScript types and interfaces, not runtime validation.

faq.question: Can it refactor existing type files?
faq.answer: It can suggest and apply modular splits when the project context is available.

faq.question: What file size rule does it follow?
faq.answer: It directs each type or interface file to stay at 50 lines or fewer.

faq.question: Is it specific to Next.js?
faq.answer: Yes. The instructions target a Next.js app and the specified types directory.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, and Claude Code use it?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists compatibility with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
